subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Marketing Research from a large public university
Undergraduate degree in Psychology with a minor in Statistics
Completed graduate coursework in survey methodology and quantitative analysis
Received training in research ethics, including CITI certification
Attended professional workshops on accessible survey design for disability research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Market Research Analyst
Role involves designing and administering surveys focused on disability and chronic illness topics
Responsible for organizing and managing large data sets collected from research participants
Assists in academic research studies and reporting at a major academic center
Supports data-driven decision making for ongoing and new research projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the academic research sector
Specializes in healthcare-related market research
Primarily serves university-based research initiatives
Focuses on public health and disability studies
Collaborates occasionally with nonprofit organizations for research partnerships

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a large academic center with over 1,000 employees
Research team consists of approximately 15 personnel, including faculty and staff
Work environment is collaborative and multidisciplinary
Regularly interacts with both research faculty and administrative staff
Participates in department-wide meetings and cross-team research discussions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Operates within the higher education and academic research sector
Focuses on health, disability, and chronic illness research
Conducts grant-funded studies and publishes findings in peer-reviewed journals
Services internal university initiatives as well as external grant-funded projects
Engages with research participants from diverse backgrounds, including people with disabilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 8 years of experience as a Market Research Analyst
Joined the current academic center 7 years ago after completing graduate studies
Spent initial 2 years as a research assistant before promotion to current role
Has participated in more than 20 research studies during tenure
Experience includes both longitudinal and cross-sectional research projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Mid-level professional with substantial independent responsibility
Often mentors new research assistants and interns on survey design
Leads smaller research projects and acts as key contributor on larger ones
Reports to a faculty principal investigator or research manager
Recognized for expertise in survey methodology within the department

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs and programs online surveys using SurveyMonkey and Qualtrics
Manages collection, cleaning, and organization of large participant data sets
Imports and visualizes research data in Tableau to create interactive dashboards
Prepares summary reports and data presentations for principal investigators
Assists in literature review and research study planning as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time on survey design and data collection
Devotes 30% of work hours to data cleaning, organization, and integration
Allocates 20% of time to data analysis and dashboard creation in Tableau
Uses about 10% of time for meetings, research planning, and literature review
Regularly balances multiple projects, adjusting proportions as deadlines approach

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has significant autonomy in selecting survey question formats and logic
Responsible for ensuring data is organized and formatted correctly for analysis
Consults with principal investigators for project scope and final survey approval
Can independently choose tools and methods for data visualization
Participates in team decisions regarding research priorities and data collection methods

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Creating survey questions and designing user-friendly survey flows
Organizing participant data in Excel, including basic cleaning and sorting
Setting up standard data visualizations in Tableau using templates
Communicating research processes and findings to team members
Adapting previous surveys for new study iterations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Managing data integration from multiple sources with inconsistent formats
Ensuring accessibility and inclusivity in survey design for disability research
Troubleshooting Tableau dashboard errors with large, complex data sets
Balancing survey brevity with sufficient data collection for meaningful analysis
Coordinating schedules and communication among large participant cohorts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Routine data cleaning and reformatting in Excel before import to Tableau
Transferring survey responses from platform exports into analysis-ready formats
Checking for missing or duplicate entries in large data sets
Creating standard summary tables for initial data review
Distributing survey invitations and reminder emails to participants

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys designing interactive dashboards and visual storytelling with Tableau
Finds satisfaction in optimizing survey flows for participant ease
Feels most engaged when interpreting results and discussing findings with the team
Considers repetitive data cleaning and manual data entry as tedious
Least enjoys administrative follow-ups, such as sending reminders or tracking responses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data cleaning is time-consuming and prone to minor errors
Survey platforms sometimes have limited export options, complicating downstream analysis
Data format inconsistencies between SurveyMonkey, Qualtrics, and Excel require extra steps
Tableau dashboards occasionally slow down with very large data sets
Coordinating participant communications can be logistically challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher skill in survey design and visualization leads to greater enjoyment and faster output
Repetitive tasks reduce motivation and slow down productivity over time
Learning new features in Tableau increases satisfaction and perceived value of work
Tasks that require creative problem-solving are both enjoyable and productive
Delegating or automating tedious processes can boost overall work satisfaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally enthusiastic about adopting new research technologies
Sees technology as a way to improve workflow efficiency
Actively seeks out best practices and innovative tools in the field
Values user-friendly tools that integrate well with existing systems
Approaches new tech with curiosity rather than apprehension

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to updates in survey platforms or data analysis tools
Comfortable exploring new features independently or with minimal guidance
Has successfully transitioned workflows from Excel-based to Tableau-based reporting
Occasionally supports peers in adopting new tools
Adapts AI-powered features into regular workflow without major difficulty

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Regularly allocates time for online tutorials and webinars on Tableau and survey platforms
Attends at least 2-3 professional development workshops or webinars per year
Prefers hands-on learning and self-paced courses for new technologies
Invests in learning tech that directly enhances daily work or research outcomes
Occasionally postpones training when faced with tight project deadlines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Most motivated when new tech promises clear workflow or data quality improvements
Barriers include high current workload and lack of immediate project relevance
Unlikely to invest time in tools that do not integrate with existing platforms
Peer recommendations often influence willingness to try new software
Prefers tech with strong documentation and community support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tool tips and shortcuts, accelerating adoption
Management encourages experimentation with AI and data visualization tools
Team culture supports sharing lessons learned from trying new platforms
Group training sessions are occasionally organized for major tool updates
Has introduced new software to the team based on positive personal experiences

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Excel extensively for data organization and preliminary analysis
Creates surveys using SurveyMonkey and Qualtrics, depending on project requirements
Designs and updates interactive dashboards in Tableau for research reporting
Leverages Microsoft Teams and Outlook for internal communication
Occasionally uses Python for advanced data manipulation tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Excel is essential for data cleaning, structuring, and error-checking
SurveyMonkey and Qualtrics provide flexible survey deployment options
Tableau is critical for data visualization and sharing results with stakeholders
Uses SharePoint to store and share research documents securely
Relies on standard office software (Word, PowerPoint) for reporting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Has advanced proficiency in Excel, including pivot tables and formulas
Skilled in using Tableau for both basic and advanced visualizations
Familiar with major survey platforms commonly used in academic research
Aware of SPSS and R, but uses them less frequently due to team preferences
Regularly keeps up with updates in main survey and visualization platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally experiences formatting issues when exporting data from SurveyMonkey to Excel
Data imports to Tableau sometimes require manual adjustment due to column mismatches
Limited native integration between survey platforms and Tableau necessitates manual steps
Version mismatches in Excel files can cause compatibility problems when collaborating
Would benefit from more seamless data syncing between survey tools and visualization platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands foundational AI concepts such as natural language processing and predictive modeling
Familiar with terms like large language models (LLMs), supervised learning, and data labeling
Reads professional articles on AI trends in research and analytics
Comfortable explaining basic AI concepts to colleagues
Recognizes the distinction between AI-powered features and traditional automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT regularly to generate survey question drafts and summarize research articles
Employs Microsoft Copilot for Excel to streamline data cleaning and formula suggestions
Has experimented with Tableau's AI-assisted analytics features for trend detection
Occasionally tries Google Bard for brainstorming survey logic and response categories
Participated in pilot testing of an AI-powered data anonymization tool

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs like ChatGPT weekly for survey design and literature summarization
Uses AI-assisted features in Excel several times per week for organizing large datasets
Employs Tableau's AI tools monthly to identify data patterns and outliers
Relies on AI suggestions to refine survey question wording for clarity and accessibility
Tests new AI-based research tools as they become available through the academic center

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated survey questions have sometimes improved clarity and participant engagement
Once used AI to summarize a large literature review, saving several hours of manual reading
Encountered inaccurate AI categorization of open-text survey responses, requiring manual correction
AI-based data cleaning suggestions occasionally missed context-specific data nuances
Learned to always verify AI output before finalizing research materials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Organization provides optional webinars on integrating AI into research workflows
Peers regularly share AI tips and findings during monthly team meetings
Internal knowledge base includes guides for using ChatGPT and Copilot in research tasks
No formal certification required for AI tool usage, but informal peer support is strong
Occasionally consults IT for troubleshooting or advanced AI tool configuration

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers a step-by-step collaborative approach when integrating AI into survey design
Often uses AI tools independently for brainstorming or summarization tasks
Seeks interactive AI guidance for complex data cleaning processes
Appreciates being able to iterate with AI tools, refining outputs incrementally
Switches between independent and collaborative modes depending on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant that accelerates repetitive tasks
Occasionally uses AI as an advisor, especially for survey wording and logic suggestions
Treats AI as a co-worker for brainstorming new research ideas or approaches
Reserves final decision-making for human team members, with AI as a supporting resource
Finds the assistant model most effective for boosting productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to AI workflows that save time or reduce manual errors
Has proactively adopted AI-driven data cleaning and survey suggestion features
Participates in internal pilots for testing new AI-powered research tools
Encourages peers to try AI-driven approaches when proven effective
Willing to adapt workflows if AI integration is seamless with existing tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and creative tasks
Finds command-based AI interfaces more efficient for structured or repetitive actions
Uses chat-based LLMs (like ChatGPT) for context-rich interactions
Switches to command-style prompts for formula generation or data transformation tasks
Appreciates hybrid interfaces that allow switching between styles as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Survey design for disability research requires nuanced understanding of accessibility and inclusivity
Data interpretation often depends on awareness of disability-related context and participant backgrounds
Certain survey logic decisions are informed by prior experience and unpublished insights
Trusts personal expertise and peer review for critical research decisions
AI is helpful for structure, but final content often requires domain-specific judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated outputs for routine data cleaning and survey draft suggestions
Exercises caution and manual review for all AI-created research materials
Rarely relies solely on AI for final survey wording or data interpretation
Views AI as a productivity tool rather than an authority on content accuracy
Trust increases as AI tools demonstrate reliability and transparency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive tasks like data cleaning and initial survey drafts
Human effort is prioritized for tasks requiring context, interpretation, or ethical judgment
Ideal workflow involves AI handling 60-70% of routine processes, with human oversight
Believes collaboration between AI and human expertise produces the best research outcomes
Regularly reviews AI outputs before incorporating them into final deliverables

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for administrative and repetitive data processing tasks
Views full automation of survey logic or participant communication as potentially risky
Insists on human review for any outputs directly affecting research quality or ethics
Would feel threatened if AI made final decisions without human transparency or override options
Supports automation when it augments rather than replaces domain expertise

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI has enhanced the importance of data literacy and critical evaluation skills
Routine technical skills (e.g., basic data cleaning) are less valuable due to automation
Skills in survey methodology and domain expertise remain highly valued
AI has shifted focus toward interpreting and contextualizing findings
Ongoing learning is necessary to maintain relevance as AI capabilities grow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Increasing need for prompt engineering and effective AI tool usage
Responsible for evaluating and validating AI-generated research outputs
Develops skills in integrating and troubleshooting AI features within existing tools
Adopts new practices for ethical use and transparency of AI in research reporting
Stays updated on AI-driven best practices for survey accessibility

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core research analysis or domain-specific tasks
Recognizes that repetitive or administrative tasks are at higher risk of automation
Believes human oversight is essential for ethical and context-sensitive research
Monitors AI developments to anticipate potential impacts on job scope
Views AI as a tool for augmentation rather than replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always performs manual checks on AI-generated survey drafts and data outputs
AI features in Tableau and Excel can be overridden or adjusted as needed
Follows organizational protocols for human review before publishing research results
Prefers tools that provide clear audit trails for all automated actions
Participates in peer review processes to catch AI-related errors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team has adopted guidelines for responsible and transparent AI use in research
Recent updates to IT policy require disclosure of AI-generated content in deliverables
Management encourages experimentation with new AI tools, within ethical boundaries
Policies emphasize the necessity of human oversight in all critical research tasks
Organization is actively reviewing best practices for AI integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will significantly accelerate research and data analysis in academia
Sees potential for AI to improve accessibility in survey design and participation
Recognizes risks of bias and misinformation if AI is misapplied
Anticipates AI will reshape job roles but not eliminate the need for human researchers
Views AI as a tool for democratizing knowledge and improving efficiency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Advocates for transparent disclosure of AI involvement in research outputs
Believes in maintaining participant confidentiality when using AI-driven tools
Cautions against over-reliance on AI for decisions affecting vulnerable populations
Supports regular review of AI tools for potential bias or unintended consequences
Values clear ethical guidelines for AI use in academic research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires more robust AI-assisted survey accessibility checkers
Would benefit from seamless integration of survey, analysis, and visualization platforms
Wants AI tools that can suggest data cleaning steps based on research context
Seeks AI features for automated literature review and citation management
Needs improved AI-powered participant communication and scheduling assistants

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects greater focus on interpretation and strategic use of AI-generated insights
Foresees routine data processing becoming almost fully automated
Predicts new responsibilities in AI oversight and ethical review
Anticipates closer collaboration between research, IT, and AI specialists
Believes continuous learning will be essential for adapting to evolving AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop clear guidelines on ethical and transparent AI use in research
Provide ongoing training and resources for new AI tools
Invest in secure, integrated platforms that support AI-driven workflows
Facilitate cross-team collaboration to share AI best practices
Implement regular review and feedback loops for AI tool effectiveness and fairness
